name: Ichigo Daifuku
texture: Soft, chewy mochi with juicy strawberry
description: A whole strawberry wrapped in smooth anko and soft mochi. The perfect balance of sweet, tart, and chewy.
where_to_try: Any department store basement (depachika) in Japan
fun_fact: Invented in the 1980s, now one of Japan's most beloved seasonal treats.
best_for: First-time wagashi experience, spring gift giving

name: Mame Daifuku
texture: Chewy mochi with crunchy red beans
description: Mochi studded with lightly salted red beans, filled with chunky tsubuan. The salt contrast makes the sweetness pop.
where_to_try: Traditional wagashiya shops nationwide
fun_fact: The salt-sweet contrast is considered the soul of Japanese confectionery.

name: Yomogi Daifuku
texture: Soft, aromatic mochi with herbal fragrance
description: Mochi infused with fragrant Japanese mugwort (yomogi), giving it a vivid green color and earthy herbal aroma.
where_to_try: Wagashiya shops, especially in spring
fun_fact: Yomogi has been used in Japanese folk medicine for centuries. In wagashi, it signals the arrival of spring.

name: Sakura Mochi (Kanto)
key_ingredient: Domyoji flour, anko, pickled cherry leaf
description: Pink-tinted thin crepe wrapped around smooth anko, finished with a pickled cherry leaf. The Kanto style uses a wheat-based skin.
where_to_try: Chomeiji temple in Tokyo, the birthplace of sakura mochi
fun_fact: First made around 1717 at Chomeiji temple using salted cherry leaves from along the Sumida River.

name: Sakura Mochi (Kansai)
key_ingredient: Domyoji flour, anko, pickled cherry leaf
description: Coarsely ground domyoji rice shaped around anko and wrapped in a pickled cherry leaf. The Kansai style has a distinctive grainy texture.
where_to_try: Any wagashiya in Kyoto or Osaka during spring
fun_fact: The debate over whether to eat the cherry leaf is a perennial topic in Japan. Both ways are correct.

name: Warabi Mochi
key_ingredient: Bracken starch, kinako (roasted soybean flour), kuromitsu
description: Translucent, impossibly soft jelly-like mochi made from bracken starch, dusted with kinako and drizzled with kuromitsu (black sugar syrup).
where_to_try: Street vendors in Kyoto and Nara, especially near temples
fun_fact: Real warabi starch is extremely rare and expensive. Most commercial versions use other starches.

name: Kuzu Mochi
description: Translucent, cool mochi made from kuzu (Japanese arrowroot) starch. Served chilled with kinako and kuromitsu.
where_to_try: Funabashiya in Tokyo for Kanto style, Yoshino area for Kansai style
fun_fact: Tokyo and Kansai kuzu mochi are completely different. Tokyo style is fermented wheat starch, while Kansai uses actual kuzu root.

name: Momiji Manju
texture: Soft castella-like cake shell, smooth filling
description: Maple leaf-shaped manju from Miyajima island. A soft castella cake shell filled with smooth anko or custard.
where_to_try: Miyajima island, Hiroshima. Freshly fried ones (age momiji) are a must.
fun_fact: Created in 1906 for visitors to Itsukushima Shrine. Over 1 billion are sold each year.

name: Onsen Manju
texture: Thin steamed skin, dense smooth filling
description: Small steamed buns with brown sugar skin and anko filling. A staple at every hot spring resort town in Japan.
where_to_try: Ikaho Onsen (Gunma) is famous as the birthplace, but every onsen town has its version
fun_fact: The brown color comes from brown sugar in the dough, not baking. Steaming gives them their signature soft texture.

name: Castella Manju
texture: Rich sponge cake exterior, smooth filling
description: A fusion of Portuguese castella sponge cake and Japanese manju. Rich egg-based cake surrounds smooth anko.
where_to_try: Bunmeido and other castella specialty shops
fun_fact: Castella itself arrived in Japan via Portuguese traders in the 16th century. Japan then reinvented it as a wagashi.
best_for: Those who enjoy Western-Japanese fusion sweets

name: Mizu Yokan
description: A lighter, more refreshing version of yokan with higher water content. Served chilled as a summer treat.
where_to_try: Toraya in Tokyo, or local wagashiya during summer months
fun_fact: In Fukui prefecture, mizu yokan is traditionally eaten in winter, not summer, as a New Year treat.

name: Neri Yokan
description: The classic, dense form of yokan. A firm block of sweetened anko set with agar. Rich and intensely sweet.
where_to_try: Toraya is the most prestigious maker, with locations in Tokyo, Kyoto, and Paris
fun_fact: Toraya has been making yokan since the 16th century. A single bar can last a year unopened, making it a perfect gift.
best_for: Formal gift giving, matcha pairing, long shelf life

name: Mushi Yokan
texture: Steamed, slightly grainy, firmer than neri yokan
description: Steamed yokan with a denser, slightly grainy texture. Often made with chestnuts (kuri mushi yokan) in autumn.
where_to_try: Seasonal wagashiya offerings in autumn
fun_fact: Mushi yokan predates neri yokan historically. The agar-set method came later as a preservation technique.

name: Mitarashi Dango
key_ingredient: Rice flour, soy sauce, sugar, mirin
description: Chewy rice dumplings on a skewer, grilled and glazed with a sweet soy sauce (shoyu) glaze. Savory-sweet perfection.
where_to_try: Kamo Mitarashi Chaya near Shimogamo Shrine in Kyoto
fun_fact: Named after the bubbles in the Mitarashi stream at Shimogamo Shrine. The original dango had 5 balls representing a human body.

name: Hanami Dango
description: Three colored dango on a skewer: pink (cherry), white (snow), and green (new leaves). The iconic cherry blossom viewing snack.
where_to_try: Any park during cherry blossom season, convenience stores in spring
fun_fact: The three colors represent spring: pink for cherry blossoms, white for lingering snow, green for the new growth beneath.

name: Tsukimi Dango
description: Plain white rice dumplings stacked in a pyramid to honor the autumn harvest moon. Simple, pure, and meditative.
where_to_try: Homemade or at wagashiya during the mid-autumn moon viewing festival
fun_fact: Traditionally 15 dango are stacked in a pyramid to match the 15th night of the 8th lunar month (Jugoya).

name: Nerikiri
key_ingredient: Shiro-an (white bean paste), gyuhi (soft mochi), food coloring
description: The pinnacle of wagashi artistry. White bean paste sculpted into seasonal shapes like flowers, leaves, and fruits. Each piece is a miniature sculpture.
where_to_try: Toraya, Tsuruya Yoshinobu in Kyoto, or any high-end wagashiya
fun_fact: A master nerikiri artisan trains for at least 10 years. Each piece reflects the exact moment of the season.
best_for: Tea ceremony, experiencing wagashi as art

name: Kinton
texture: Fluffy, thread-like strands over smooth core
description: Delicate threads of sweetened bean paste wrapped around an anko core. Colors shift with the seasons, from spring pastels to autumn golds.
where_to_try: High-end wagashiya in Kyoto, department store depachika
fun_fact: The thread-like texture is created by pressing the paste through a fine mesh sieve. No two kinton look exactly alike.

name: Classic Monaka
texture: Crispy wafer shell, dense chewy filling
description: Thin crispy mochi wafer shells sandwiching dense anko. The contrast between the fragile shell and rich filling is distinctive.
where_to_try: Kuya in Ginza (Tokyo) is legendary for their monaka
fun_fact: The name monaka means 'the middle of the moon' and the wafers were originally round like the full moon.
best_for: Gift giving, anko lovers, shelf-stable wagashi

name: Ice Cream Monaka
texture: Crispy wafer, cold creamy ice cream
key_ingredient: Mochi wafer, vanilla ice cream, anko (sometimes)
description: Ice cream sandwiched between crispy monaka wafers. A beloved convenience store and supermarket frozen treat.
where_to_try: Any convenience store or supermarket in Japan
fun_fact: The most famous brand, Yukimi Daifuku by Lotte, sells over 400 million units annually in Japan.
best_for: Casual summer snacking, convenience store exploring

name: Classic Dorayaki
description: Two fluffy honey-infused pancakes sandwiching chunky tsubuan. Japan's most accessible wagashi, loved by all ages.
where_to_try: Usagiya in Ueno (Tokyo) is legendary. Convenience stores carry good everyday versions.
fun_fact: Doraemon's favorite food. The anime character single-handedly made dorayaki famous worldwide.
best_for: Kids, first-time wagashi eaters, everyday snack

name: Matcha Dorayaki
texture: Fluffy matcha pancakes, smooth white anko
description: Matcha-infused pancakes with white bean paste filling. The slight bitterness of matcha balances the sweetness beautifully.
where_to_try: Specialty wagashiya and depachika
fun_fact: Matcha dorayaki became popular as Japan's matcha boom expanded beyond tea into every possible food.

name: Cream Dorayaki
texture: Fluffy pancakes, anko with whipped cream
description: A modern take on dorayaki with whipped cream layered alongside the anko. Richer and more indulgent than the classic.
where_to_try: Asakusa and depachika nationwide
fun_fact: The nama (fresh cream) dorayaki boom started in the 2010s and now outsells classic dorayaki in many shops.

name: Rakugan
description: Pressed dry sweets made from wasanbon sugar and rice flour. Pressed into molds depicting seasonal motifs. Dissolve like snowflakes on your tongue.
where_to_try: Moroeya in Kanazawa, high-end tea ceremony supply shops
fun_fact: Kanazawa is Japan's rakugan capital. The Kaga domain promoted wagashi culture to rival Kyoto's sophistication.

name: Konpeito
description: Tiny star-shaped sugar candies in rainbow colors. Originally introduced by Portuguese missionaries in the 16th century.
where_to_try: Ryokujuan Shimizu in Kyoto has made them since 1847
fun_fact: Each konpeito takes 14-20 days to make, slowly building sugar layers in a rotating copper pot. Ryokujuan makes only one flavor per season.

name: Wasanbon
description: The finest pressed sugar sweets, made from wasanbon sugar unique to Shikoku. Melt on the tongue with an incredibly delicate sweetness.
where_to_try: Baiikoudo in Kagawa, specialty shops in Shikoku
fun_fact: Wasanbon sugar is hand-kneaded (te-momi) to remove molasses, a process repeated three times (san-bon means three steps).

name: Nure Senbei
description: Intentionally soft, moist rice crackers brushed with soy sauce while still warm. Unlike regular senbei, these are chewy, not crunchy.
where_to_try: Choshi Dentetsu railway stations in Chiba sell the most famous version
fun_fact: Choshi Dentetsu railway was saved from bankruptcy by the success of their nure senbei sales. Buying them literally keeps the trains running.
best_for: Savory snack lovers, supporting local railways

name: Taiyaki
description: Fish-shaped (sea bream) cake filled with hot anko. Crispy on the outside, warm and gooey inside. Best eaten from the tail.
where_to_try: Naniwaya Sohonten in Azabu-Juban (Tokyo), the original taiyaki shop since 1909
fun_fact: The debate over whether to eat from the head or the tail is a classic Japanese conversation starter. Head-first eaters are said to be bold.

name: Imagawayaki
description: Round, thick pancake-like cakes filled generously with anko. Similar to taiyaki but round and with thicker batter.
where_to_try: Street stalls and festival vendors nationwide
fun_fact: Called by different names across Japan: imagawayaki (Tokyo), obanyaki (Kansai), kaiten-yaki (Kyushu). The name debate never ends.

name: Anmitsu
key_ingredient: Agar jelly, anko, fruits, mochi, kuromitsu
description: A parfait-like dessert of agar jelly cubes topped with anko, fresh fruits, mochi balls, and kuromitsu syrup. Cool and refreshing.
where_to_try: Mihashi in Ueno (Tokyo), open since 1948
fun_fact: Anmitsu originated in the early Showa period as an upgrade to mitsumame by adding anko (an + mitsu).
best_for: Summer dessert, those who want variety in one bowl

name: Zenzai
texture: Thick sweet soup with chewy mochi
description: A warm, thick sweet soup made from azuki beans, served with toasted mochi. The ultimate Japanese winter comfort food.
where_to_try: Funawaya in Tokyo, teahouses in Kyoto, or any kissaten in winter
fun_fact: Called zenzai in Kansai and oshiruko in Kanto. The distinction between the two is hotly debated and varies by region.
best_for: Winter warmth, comfort food, temple visits

name: Kashiwa Mochi
description: Mochi filled with anko, wrapped in an oak (kashiwa) leaf. Traditionally eaten on Children's Day (May 5th).
where_to_try: Any wagashiya or supermarket around May 5th
fun_fact: Oak leaves do not fall until new leaves grow, symbolizing family continuity. That is why kashiwa mochi is tied to Children's Day.
best_for: Children's Day celebration, understanding ritual wagashi

name: Ohagi / Botamochi
texture: Coarse, grainy rice with thick anko coating
description: A ball of roughly mashed glutinous rice coated in thick anko. Called ohagi in autumn (named after bush clover) and botamochi in spring (named after peony).
where_to_try: Nakamuraya in Tokyo, any supermarket during equinox week
fun_fact: Same wagashi, different names by season: botamochi in spring and ohagi in autumn, each named after a seasonal flower.
best_for: Equinox celebrations, understanding Japanese seasonal naming
